target.title: fix(/v1/responses): drop the extra phantom assistant turn on client-tool calls
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- **Problem:** Two issues affected `/v1/responses` client-tool calls (tools the API caller passes in `tools: [...]` for the agent to delegate back to them):
  - **Bug:** every client-tool call shipped an extra phantom assistant turn alongside the real `function_call`. **Symptom:** the model first denies it can run the tool, then seconds later in the same SSE response claims it did — a self-contradictory mini-transcript every caller saw, regardless of how their parser was built.
  - **Separately, some clients didn't see the args at all.** The gateway delivered them by populating `item.arguments` on the `output_item.added` / `output_item.done` events, instead of using the dedicated `response.function_call_arguments.delta` / `.done` events that the Responses-API streaming convention reserves for incremental function-call arguments. Clients whose parsers follow that convention strictly — only reading args from those dedicated events — saw `arguments == "{}"` for every call, because the dedicated events simply weren't being emitted. More permissive clients (e.g. OpenAI's Node SDK, which also reads `item.arguments` directly) recovered the args from the bundled-in shape and worked anyway.
  Two unrelated root causes combined to produce these symptoms. (1) The Pi-side adapter for client-tool definitions returned a placeholder stub from its `execute()` callback without `terminate: true`, so Pi treated the stub as a normal tool result and ran *another* model turn before the runner observed the handoff — that extra turn is what leaked into the stream as the contradictory phantom narration. (2) The `/v1/responses` SSE emitter never emitted the dedicated `response.function_call_arguments.delta` / `.done` events.
- **Why it matters:** Together, the two issues meant `/v1/responses` couldn't be relied on for client-tool calls — every caller (lenient or strict) saw the conversation contaminated by the contradictory phantom narration, and on top of that, strict clients lost the args entirely.
- **What changed:** The Pi adapter for client-tool definitions now returns `terminate: true` on its handoff stub, so Pi stops the agent loop after this batch and the placeholder stub does not produce another model turn. Piggybacked on top of that, the gateway's SSE event sequence for streamed function_call output items is tightened to follow the Responses-API streaming convention more closely (`output_item.added` with empty placeholder → `function_call_arguments.delta` → `function_call_arguments.done` → `output_item.done` with populated args), and the upstream Responses-stream parser also gains a handler for `response.function_call_arguments.done` to cover the case where an upstream delivers the full args only via `.done`.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** No changes to the non-streaming JSON response path. No changes to non-`/v1/responses` endpoints. No changes to authentication, secrets handling, command/tool execution surface, or data access scope. No changes to `DefaultConfigurationExtension.threadTransformers` seeding or to any plugin contract.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- **Root cause:** Two independent root causes in two layers.
  1. The Pi adapter at `src/agents/pi-tool-definition-adapter.ts` returned a placeholder stub for client-tool calls without setting Pi's `terminate: true` flag, so Pi treated the stub as a normal tool result and ran an extra model turn before the outer runner observed `clientToolCallDetected`. That extra turn is what produced the contradictory phantom narration in the SSE stream.
  2. The `/v1/responses` SSE emitter at `src/gateway/openresponses-http.ts` shipped streamed function-call args by populating `item.arguments` on the `output_item.added` / `output_item.done` events instead of using the dedicated `response.function_call_arguments.delta` / `.done` events. The Responses-API streaming convention reserves those dedicated events for incremental args; some client parsers (those that read args only from those events) saw nothing because they weren't emitted.
- **Missing detection / guardrail:** No regression test asserted that the adapter's `execute()` callback returned `terminate: true`, and no regression test pinned the four-event SSE sequence for streamed function_calls. Both are now covered.
- **Contributing context (if known):** The Responses-API streaming convention pairs the bookend `output_item.{added,done}` events with dedicated `function_call_arguments.{delta,done}` events that carry the streamed args; `openresponses-http.ts` had only ever emitted the bookends. The OpenAI Node SDK and pi-ai both seed their args buffer from `output_item.added.item.arguments` and then append each `delta`, so they recovered the args from the bundled-in shape; parsers that read args only from the dedicated events did not.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- **Target test or file:**
  - `src/agents/pi-tool-definition-adapter.test.ts` — pins the `terminate: true` flag on the handoff stub returned by the wrapped client-tool `execute()` callback.
  - `src/gateway/openresponses-http.test.ts` — pins the SSE event sequence for streamed function_call output items.
  - `src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts` — pins the `.done`-only-args recovery branch in the upstream Responses-stream parser.
- **Scenario the test should lock in:** The Pi adapter's `execute()` callback returns `terminate: true` so the agent loop stops after the handoff; the `/v1/responses` SSE stream for a streamed `function_call` carries the dedicated `function_call_arguments.{delta,done}` events between the `output_item.added` and `output_item.done` bookends; and `processResponsesStream` recovers args from `function_call_arguments.done` when no intermediate deltas were emitted.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** All three are unit-level seams over the exact code paths involved (the adapter's execute callback, the SSE emitter, and the upstream stream parser). They are deterministic, fast, and survive refactoring of internal helpers.
- **Existing test that already covers this (if any):** None.
- **If no new test is added, why not:** N/A — three new tests added.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

External clients consuming `/v1/responses` for client-tool calls will no longer see the conversation contaminated by a self-contradictory phantom assistant turn (where the model first claimed it could not run the tool, then seconds later claimed it did). On top of that, clients whose parsers read streamed args only from the dedicated `function_call_arguments.{delta,done}` events will now receive the parsed function-call arguments through those events instead of an empty `{}`. No CLI/UI/default-config changes.

## Diagram (if applicable)

**Pi agent loop (the main fix):**

```text
Before:
  model emits function_call(tool=client_tool, args={...})
  Pi → wrapped execute(args) → returns { status:"pending", ... }   (no terminate flag)
  Pi treats stub as normal tool result → runs ANOTHER model turn
  model now has the stub in context → narrates "can't run that tool"
                                          → seconds later → "actually did run it"
  loop finally returns → runner observes clientToolCallDetected
  → both the real function_call AND the contradictory narration go on the wire

After:
  model emits function_call(tool=client_tool, args={...})
  Pi → wrapped execute(args) → returns { status:"pending", ..., terminate: true }
  Pi sees terminate=true → STOPS the loop after this batch
  runner observes clientToolCallDetected → surfaces the real function_call via pendingToolCalls
  → only the real function_call goes on the wire, no narration
```

**`/v1/responses` SSE event sequence (the piggybacked wire-shape improvement):**

```text
Before:
client POST /v1/responses (stream=true, tools=[client_tool])
  -> SSE: output_item.added       (item.arguments = "<full args>")
  -> SSE: output_item.done        (item.arguments = "<full args>")
  -> client parser sees arguments == "{}"   (only if it ignores item.arguments)

After:
client POST /v1/responses (stream=true, tools=[client_tool])
  -> SSE: output_item.added                  (item.arguments = "")
  -> SSE: function_call_arguments.delta      (full args string)
  -> SSE: function_call_arguments.done       (full args string)
  -> SSE: output_item.done                   (item.arguments populated)
  -> all parsers see parsed args             ← correct
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No` — same client-tool handoff contract, just delivered through a more usual SSE event sequence.
- Data access scope changed? `No`
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: any (issue is platform-agnostic)
- Runtime/container: OpenClaw self-hosted gateway via the standard docker-compose deployment
- Model/provider: provider-agnostic — reproduces with any model that triggers a client-tool call
- Integration/channel (if any): `/v1/responses` HTTP endpoint, client-tool definition handed off to the API caller
- Relevant config (redacted): gateway with `/v1/responses` enabled and at least one agent configured

### Steps

1. Run the OpenClaw gateway with at least one agent that exposes the `/v1/responses` endpoint.
2. From any external HTTP client (curl or any OpenAI-Responses-compatible client), POST to `/v1/responses` with `stream: true` and a `tools: [...]` array including a client-tool definition.
3. Cause the model to call that client tool with non-trivial arguments.
4. Observe the conversation transcript and the parsed function-call arguments on the client side.

### Expected

- No extra assistant-text turn narrating the placeholder stub appears alongside the real `function_call`. The transcript is exactly what the caller asked for: the model emits the tool call and the agent loop yields control back for the client to handle it.
- The client receives the parsed function-call arguments matching what the model produced (delivered through the dedicated `function_call_arguments.{delta,done}` SSE events for parsers that read from those, or via `item.arguments` on the bookend events for parsers that read from there).

### Actual

- Matches Expected with this PR. Without it: a self-contradictory assistant narration appears alongside the real `function_call` (the model first declares it cannot run the tool, then a few seconds later claims it did), and clients that read args only from the dedicated streaming events see `arguments == "{}"`.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after — three regression tests added in this PR, RED before each respective fix, GREEN after (`openresponses-http.test.ts`, `pi-tool-definition-adapter.test.ts`, `openai-transport-stream.test.ts`).
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What I personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- **Verified scenarios:**
  - Ran the OpenClaw gateway locally and drove a real `/v1/responses` request from a local client that triggered a client-tool call. The client tool received its parsed arguments correctly, and the conversation contained no phantom narration of the placeholder stub alongside the real `function_call`.
- **Edge cases checked:** N/A — the regression tests pin the wire-shape invariants for any args payload, and the scenario above exercises the real handoff loop.
- **What I did not verify:** nothing scope-relevant beyond the above.

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes` — additive on the wire (the dedicated `function_call_arguments.{delta,done}` events are new; `item.arguments` on `output_item.done` still carries the populated args; only `output_item.added`'s `item.arguments` field changes from `<full args>` to `""`).
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** A client that read `item.arguments` from `output_item.added` as its sole source (and ignored both the dedicated args events and `output_item.done`'s `item.arguments`) will now read `""`.
  - **Mitigation:** This is an uncommon parser pattern — neither the OpenAI Node SDK nor pi-ai work that way; both also consume the dedicated streaming events and `output_item.done.item.arguments`. The populated args remain available via both of those alternative sources for any client that follows either pattern.

---

## AI-assisted PR

- [x] Mark as AI-assisted in the PR title or description
- [x] Note the degree of testing: **fully tested** (three RED/GREEN regression tests added in this PR; CONTRIBUTING.md gates `pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test` run on a Linux box matching CI conditions; touched files clean for `pnpm lint` and `pnpm format:check`).
- Session logs: developed iteratively with Claude Opus 4.7 via Claude Code in the OpenClaw repo.
- [x] I understand what the code does — see the per-file commit body for the rationale at each touch point.

target.title: fix: route wake hooks to explicit sessions
target.body:
## Summary
- let direct `/hooks/wake` payloads carry an optional `sessionKey`
- honor `action: "wake"` mapping `sessionKey` / `agentId` routing instead of always waking `main`
- extend wake-hook tests and config guards so templated wake session keys follow the same policy as agent hooks

## Why
The current gateway silently ignores wake-hook session targeting and always enqueues into the default main session, even when the operator provided a wake session key or configured a wake mapping with agent/session routing.

This is what bit the GitHub webhook relay dogfood path: the relay could wake Howard, but not the intended existing session, so the signal was quieter than intended and could drift into the wrong place.

Fixes #64556.

## Notes
- mapped wake hooks with `agentId` and no explicit `sessionKey` now target that agent's main session instead of falling back to the default main agent session
- direct `/hooks/wake` treats `sessionKey` as an exact explicit target and does **not** do agent rebinding; mapped wake keeps the existing target-agent normalization path

## Validation
- `corepack pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/hooks.test.ts src/gateway/hooks-mapping.test.ts src/gateway/server.hooks.test.ts`
- pre-commit `check:changed` gate (including typecheck, lint, import-cycle checks, and the gateway test shard)
- Cursor Composer (`composer-2-fast`) second-mind review on the final diff: ship, with the config-guard gap fixed before opening

target.title: add option for MCP apps support
target.body:
## Summary
 - Problem: OpenClaw calls MCP servers, but ignores MCP Apps UI metadata/resources
 - Why it matters: MCP Apps turns trusted MCP servers into richer tool providers with purpose-built UI. Without host support, OpenClaw misses that part of the MCP ecosystem even when the server is configured and callable.
- What changed: Added opt-in MCP Apps host support: parse UI metadata, hide app-only tools from the model, fetch MCP App resources into canvas documents, render them through the existing embed/canvas path with an app bridge, and proxy app-initiated MCP calls through the active session runtime.
- What changed: Added restart/reload hardening for restored app views, wider assistant canvas layout, docs, config schema support,tests, and a security-audit warning when `mcp.apps.enabled=true`.
-  What did NOT change (scope boundary): Normal MCP tool execution still works without MCP Apps. MCP Apps are default-off behind `mcp.apps.enabled`; this does not add a marketplace, new MCP server examples, or plugin-only architecture.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)
- MCP app-only tools are not materialized for the model.
- MCP Apps UI payloads are only fetched/rendered when `mcp.apps.enabled=true`.
- App bridge calls are rejected when MCP Apps are disabled.
- App bridge calls enforce app-callable tool visibility.
- Persisted/restored app views can recreate an MCP runtime after gateway restart.
- Existing `[embed]` canvas rendering remains the fallback path when MCP Apps metadata is absent or disabled.

- Target test or file:
  - New opt-in config: `mcp.apps.enabled`.
  - Default behavior is unchanged for normal MCP tools; MCP Apps UI rendering is disabled by default.
  - When enabled, trusted MCP servers can render interactive app views in chat using the existing canvas/embed path.
  - MCP Apps views may call app-visible MCP tools through the active session runtime.
  - App-only MCP tools are hidden from the model.
  - `openclaw security audit` now warns when MCP Apps are enabled.
  - Docs now describe MCP Apps setup, scope, and security considerations. 
- Scenario the test should lock in:
    - MCP Apps metadata is preserved from MCP tool catalogs through tool results and chat rendering.
    - App-only tools are hidden from the model.
    - Model-only tools are blocked from app bridge calls.
    - MCP Apps UI rendering and bridge proxy calls require `mcp.apps.enabled=true`.
    - Restored app views can recreate an MCP runtime after gateway restart.
    - Existing canvas/embed rendering remains the fallback when MCP Apps are disabled or metadata is absent.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:
    - The feature crosses runtime materialization, gateway proxying, config/bootstrap, and UI rendering. Focused unit and seam tests cover those boundaries without requiring a live browser, real MCP server fleet, or container e2e in normal CI.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any):
    - Existing canvas/embed tests covered generic iframe rendering, but not MCP Apps metadata, app-only visibility, app bridge calls, or restored MCP runtime behavior.

  ## User-visible / Behavior Changes
  - New opt-in config: `mcp.apps.enabled`.
  - Default behavior is unchanged for normal MCP tools; MCP Apps UI rendering is disabled by default.
  - When enabled, trusted MCP servers can render interactive app views in chat using the existing canvas/embed path.
  - MCP Apps views may call app-visible MCP tools through the active session runtime.
  - App-only MCP tools are hidden from the model.
  - `openclaw security audit` now warns when MCP Apps are enabled.
  - Docs now describe MCP Apps setup, scope, and security considerations.
  
## Diagram (if applicable)

  ```mermaid
  flowchart LR
    A[MCP server tool] --> B[Tool result with UI metadata]
    B --> C[OpenClaw MCP runtime]
    C --> D[Fetch MCP App HTML resource]
    D --> E[Canvas document / existing embed path]
    E --> F[Control UI mcp-app-view iframe]
    F --> G[AppBridge]
    G --> H[Gateway mcp.* proxy]
    H --> C
  ```
## Security Impact (required)
  - New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): Yes
  - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
  - New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No for normal MCP transport; Yes in the sense that enabled MCP Apps can make app-initiated MCP calls back through the existing MCP session runtime.
  - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): Yes
  - Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): Yes
  - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
    - Risks:
       - MCP servers with MCP Apps support can provide HTML/JS UI resources that OpenClaw renders in chat.
       -  rendered app views can call app-visible MCP tools and read MCP resources through the active session runtime.
       - app views receive the tool input/result payload they are rendering.
    - Mitigations:
        - MCP Apps are default-off behind `mcp.apps.enabled=true`.
        - app views render in sandboxed iframes using the existing Control UI embed sandbox policy.
        - app-only tools are hidden from the model, and model-only tools are blocked from app bridge calls.
        - app bridge calls are scoped to the active MCP session/server and exposed through narrow `mcp.*` gateway methods.
        - `openclaw security audit` warns when MCP Apps are enabled.
- Trust model: enabling MCP Apps means trusting the configured MCP servers not only as tool providers, but also as UI code providers.

  ## Repro + Verification

  ### Environment
  - OS: macOS
  - Runtime/container: local OpenClaw container via Podman
  - Model/provider: Claude Sonnet 4.6
  - Integration/channel (if any): Control UI chat with configured MCP servers
  - Relevant config (redacted):

```json
  {
    "mcp": {
      "apps": {
        "enabled": true
      },
      "servers": {
        "system-monitor": {
          "command": "npx",
          "args": ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-system-monitor", "--stdio"]
        },
        "pdf": {
          "command": "npx",
          "args": ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-pdf", "--stdio"]
        },
        "qr": {
          "url": "http://127.0.0.1:3108/mcp",
          "transport": "streamable-http"
        }
      }
    }
  }
```

### Steps
  1. Build and run an OpenClaw image from this branch.
  2. Enable MCP Apps with openclaw config set mcp.apps.enabled true --strict-json.
  3. Configure the system-monitor, pdf, and qr MCP servers.
  4. Start the QR MCP sidecar if using the QR example.
  5. Start a fresh chat/session in the Control UI.
  6. Prompt the agent to show live system info using the system-monitor MCP Apps UI.
  7. Prompt the agent to generate a QR code using the QR MCP Apps UI.
  8. Prompt the agent to display https://arxiv.org/pdf/1706.03762 using the PDF MCP Apps UI.
  9. Restart the OpenClaw container and reload the Control UI.
  10. Confirm the persisted PDF app view reloads and reconnects.

### Expected

  - Normal MCP tools still work.
  - With mcp.apps.enabled=true, MCP Apps UI resources render as interactive embedded app views.
  - System monitor renders a live monitor UI.
  - QR renders an embedded QR UI.
  - PDF renders an interactive PDF viewer.
  - Restored app views can reconnect after gateway/container restart.
  - With mcp.apps.enabled=false, app bridge calls are rejected and MCP Apps render through the normal fallback path instead of the app bridge.

### Actual

  - Verified locally with system-monitor, QR, and PDF MCP Apps examples.
  - Verified upstream image comparison: upstream can call the MCP servers but does not render the MCP Apps UI resources.
  - Verified restart/reload behavior for the PDF app view.

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [x] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/aaa93975-4406-493e-a060-4ac64763704b

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:
    - No migration is required for existing installs. MCP Apps support is default-off.
    - To enable it, set:
      ```bash
      openclaw config set mcp.apps.enabled true --strict-json
      ```
    - Configure MCP servers that expose MCP Apps / `mcp-ui` resources.
    - Restart OpenClaw so the updated config is loaded.

## Risks and Mitigations

  - Risk: Enabling MCP Apps allows configured MCP servers to provide HTML/JS UI that renders inside OpenClaw.
    - Mitigation: Feature is opt-in via `mcp.apps.enabled`; app UI renders in a sandboxed iframe; docs and security audit call out the trust boundary.
  - Risk: App views can call app-visible MCP tools/resources through the bridge, increasing the practical surface area of a trusted MCP server.
    - Mitigation: Bridge is scoped to MCP app methods only; model-only tools are blocked from app bridge calls; app-only tools are hidden from the model.
  - Risk: Restored chat views may need to reconnect to MCP runtimes after a container restart.
    - Mitigation: Gateway proxy lazily recreates the session MCP runtime from config when an app bridge call arrives and no runtime is currently active.
  - Risk: Servers without MCP Apps support, or installs with the feature disabled, should not regress.
    - Mitigation: Existing MCP tool execution path remains unchanged; UI falls back to the existing embed/canvas rendering behavior when MCP Apps are disabled or metadata is absent.

target.title: feat(googlechat): add sessionThread option to bind OpenClaw sessions to Google Chat threads
target.body: AI-assisted: yes (Claude Opus 4.7). ## Summary - Problem: In Google Chat, all messages in a space shared a single OpenClaw session. Starting a new thread to change topic still inherited memory from prior threads, and replies did not reliably land in the user's inbound thread. - Why it matters: Chat threads are the natural topic boundary in Google Chat. Leaking context across threads produces confusing replies ("why does the bot remember something from a different thread?"), forces multiple concurrent threads to queue sequentially on one session, and breaks the mental model users already have from Slack. Requested in #13373. - What changed: Added an opt-in `sessionThread` channel option. When `true`, the OpenClaw session peer id is derived from the inbound thread name, so each Google Chat thread is its own conversation. Replies are now threaded via `thread.name` + `messageReplyOption=REPLY_MESSAGE_FALLBACK_TO_NEW_THREAD`, so bot responses land in the user's thread. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): Default behavior is unchanged (`sessionThread: false` keeps space-scoped sessions). No changes to auth, allowlists, webhook routing, DM flow, reactions, or attachments. No slash commands / `/focus`-style bindings added (see "future work" below). ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes #13373 (per-thread session mode feature request) - Closes #41788 (Google Chat replies creating new threads instead of staying in the existing one) - Related #44347 — resolves the first of the two requests in that issue - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ### Note on naming vs the issue's proposal The issue proposes `threadSessionMode: "per-thread" | "per-space"`. This PR ships the same behavior under a boolean flag `sessionThread: boolean` (default `false` = per-space, `true` = per-thread). Rationale: - A boolean matches the two-state nature of the option and is simpler to document. - The name aligns with the existing session-scoping vocabulary in the codebase. - Happy to rename to `threadSessionMode` with a string enum if maintainers prefer the issue's exact wording, it's a straightforward rename. ## Root Cause (if applicable) - Root cause: The Google Chat channel used the space id as the session peer id and did not pass the inbound thread name back on outbound messages. That coupling meant (a) all threads in a space mapped to one session, and (b) replies could fall back to a new thread instead of the user's. - Missing detection / guardrail: No peer-id unit coverage for Google Chat; Slack already has `prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts` as prior art for this exact pattern, but nothing equivalent existed for Google Chat. - Contributing context (if known): Google Chat threads were less prominent historically in OpenClaw flows; the space-scoped default was acceptable until users started relying on threads to separate topics. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [ ] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: `extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.session-thread.test.ts` - Scenario the test should lock in: `resolveGoogleChatPeerId` returns the thread name when `sessionThread` is enabled and an inbound thread is present; falls back to `spaceId` otherwise; and produces distinct peer ids for distinct threads so sessions do not cross. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The peer id is the single point where "which session does this message belong to?" is decided for Google Chat. Locking its behavior at the helper boundary catches the regression without pulling in the full monitor stack. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): None for Google Chat. Slack's `prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts` covers the analogous primitive. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - New opt-in config: `channels.googlechat.sessionThread` (default `false`). - `false` (default): space-scoped sessions, prior behavior unchanged. - `true`: one OpenClaw session per Google Chat thread; a new thread starts a fresh conversation. - Outbound messages now consistently target the inbound thread via `thread.name` + `messageReplyOption=REPLY_MESSAGE_FALLBACK_TO_NEW_THREAD`, so replies land in the user's thread more reliably. This applies regardless of `sessionThread`. ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text sessionThread: false (default, unchanged) space S, thread A -> session(peer=S) space S, thread B -> session(peer=S) # shared memory sessionThread: true space S, thread A -> session(peer=A) space S, thread B -> session(peer=B) # isolated memory space S, no thread -> session(peer=S) # falls back to space-scoped ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? No - Secrets/tokens handling changed? No - New/changed network calls? No (same Chat API endpoints; only request payload now sets `thread.name` and `messageReplyOption`) - Command/tool execution surface changed? No - Data access scope changed? No (in fact, `sessionThread: true` narrows memory scope per thread) ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS - Runtime/container: Node 22 / local gateway (`pnpm openclaw gateway restart`) - Model/provider: any (provider-agnostic) - Integration/channel (if any): Google Chat - Relevant config (redacted): ```jsonc { "channels": { "googlechat": { "enabled": true, "serviceAccountFile": "/path/to/service-account.json", "sessionThread": true } } } ``` ### Steps 1. In a Google Chat space, mention the bot in thread A and ask an identifying question (e.g. "remember the color red"). 2. Start a new thread B in the same space and ask "what color did I mention?". 3. Reply again inside thread A and ask the same question. ### Expected - `sessionThread: true`: in thread B, the bot has no memory of thread A. In thread A, the bot still remembers "red". Replies land in the thread the user messaged in. - `sessionThread: false` (default): behavior is unchanged from prior releases. ### Actual - Matches expected for both modes. ## Evidence - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after ``` pnpm test extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.session-thread.test.ts ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns spaceId when sessionThread is disabled ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns spaceId when sessionThread is undefined (default) ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns thread name when sessionThread is enabled and inbound has a thread ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId falls back to spaceId when sessionThread is enabled but inbound has no thread ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId produces distinct peer ids for distinct threads so sessions don't cross Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 5 passed (5) ``` - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - When disabled(previous behavior): - When enabled: - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) - Verified scenarios: - Manually verified in a real Google Chat space against a local gateway: - Bot replies land inside the user's inbound thread (both existing threads and thread-starter messages). - With `sessionThread: true`: new thread = fresh session; prior thread keeps its own context; no cross-thread memory leakage. - With `sessionThread: false`: unchanged, space-scoped memory. - Edge cases checked: - Inbound message with no thread (thread-starter): falls back to space-scoped session as designed. - Two concurrent threads in the same space maintain separate sessions. - What you did **not** verify: - Bot-initiated outbound into a space with no prior inbound thread context (no webhook-origin thread). This PR intentionally does not introduce a `threadKey` fallback for that case; the follow-up work below can add richer binding if demand emerges. - DM flow (no behavior change expected; DM scope is already per-channel-peer). ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? Yes - Config/env changes? Yes, additive only: new optional `channels.googlechat.sessionThread` boolean (default `false`). - Migration needed? No. Existing configs continue to behave as before. - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A. To opt in, set `channels.googlechat.sessionThread: true`. ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: Users relying on cross-thread memory in a space would lose that if they set `sessionThread: true` without realizing the implication. - Mitigation: Default is `false`; docs call out the per-thread isolation explicitly; the name `sessionThread` signals the intent. - Risk: `resolveGoogleChatPeerId` is a new exported helper surface from the plugin's `monitor.ts`. - Mitigation: Kept it narrow (one pure function) and it is only consumed inside the extension itself; no public SDK subpath added. ## Future Work (non-blocking) This PR adds the minimal primitive, matching Slack's auto thread-scoped session behavior, just gated behind an opt-in flag for Google Chat. A follow-up could align with the Telegram/Discord `session.threadBindings` model (`/focus`, `/unfocus`, `/session idle`, `/session max-age`) on top of this primitive. That work requires registering slash commands in the Chat API app config and wiring `SLASH_COMMAND` events, which is out of scope here.

target.title: [codex] Cron: repair empty sanitized final replies
target.body:
﻿???## Summary

- Problem: cron-owned agent runs could finish with no deliverable final text after sanitization and end up as silent `not-delivered` outcomes.
- Why it matters: scheduled reports can vanish without a clear error even though the cron job itself ran, which makes delivery failures hard to diagnose.
- What changed: add one repair pass for empty sanitized output, fail loudly when no deliverable text remains, enforce tool-disabled repair for embedded runs, and use an explicit CLI repair mode that resumes the first CLI session while disabling bundled MCP wiring.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this PR does not relax reasoning-tag sanitization, does not change normal non-cron agent turns, and does not address the legacy local WhatsApp health-check cron issue tracked separately in #60204.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Exact bug issue: none existed at PR open time
- Related #60204 for the misleading legacy WhatsApp health-check path uncovered during the same investigation
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: cron delivery could receive an empty final text after sanitization (for example when malformed reasoning tags consumed the visible reply), but the cron runner had no recovery pass and the delivery path did not convert that condition into a hard error.
- Missing detection / guardrail: the runner did not retry with a constrained no-tools finalization prompt, and the direct-delivery path treated missing synthesized text too softly.
- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): the existing sanitization behavior is intentional; the gap was in cron finalization and delivery handling, not in the sanitizer itself.
- Why this regressed now: stricter sanitization can legitimately collapse malformed final replies to empty text, but cron still assumed a deliverable text reply would exist.
- If unknown, what was ruled out: ruled out transport-only failure for the investigated case; the actionable gap here was the empty-output cron path.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/cron/isolated-agent/run.interim-retry.test.ts`, `src/cron/isolated-agent/run.owner-auth.test.ts`, `src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: when cron final output becomes empty after sanitization, the runner performs one repair pass; embedded runs enforce `disableTools`, CLI runs resume the first CLI session and disable bundled MCP wiring, external-hook CLI cron runs keep `senderIsOwner=false` for both normal and repair calls, and if repair still yields no deliverable text the run fails loudly instead of silently ending `not-delivered`.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug lives in cron run finalization plus delivery dispatch, and the narrowest stable guardrail is to exercise those exact seams with mocked agent output.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing interim-retry coverage already covered acknowledgement retries but not empty sanitized output.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Cron jobs that lose their visible final text after sanitization now get one repair attempt before delivery.
- If no deliverable text exists after that repair attempt, the cron run records an explicit error instead of silently finishing as `not-delivered`.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[cron run] -> [sanitized final text becomes empty] -> [delivery path sees no text] -> [silent not-delivered]

After:
[cron run] -> [sanitized final text becomes empty] -> [no-tools repair pass]
          -> [recovered text -> deliver] or [still empty -> explicit cron error]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: the embedded repair path enforces `disableTools`; the CLI repair path is explicitly not represented as native-tool-free, resumes the first CLI session, and disables OpenClaw-bundled MCP wiring. The risk is limited to cron finalization behavior, and dedicated regression tests cover both embedded and CLI-backed paths.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Ubuntu Linux
- Runtime/container: local Node + pnpm checkout
- Model/provider: mocked embedded + CLI runner paths in targeted tests
- Integration/channel (if any): cron + Telegram announce path in regression coverage
- Relevant config (redacted): default test fixtures

### Steps

1. Run `pnpm test -- src/cron/isolated-agent/run.interim-retry.test.ts`
2. Run `pnpm test -- src/cron/isolated-agent.skips-delivery-without-whatsapp-recipient-besteffortdeliver-true.test.ts`
3. Run `pnpm test -- src/agents/cli-runner.prepare.test.ts`

### Expected

- Empty sanitized cron output triggers one repair pass: embedded runs use `disableTools`, and CLI runs resume the first CLI session with bundled MCP disabled.
- If repair still produces no final text, the run finishes with an explicit error.
- CLI-backed repair mode does not inject bundled MCP overlays.

### Actual

- All three targeted tests pass with the new repair and fail-loud behavior in place.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: reviewed the cron finalization and delivery diff, ran the targeted regression tests locally, confirmed CLI repair resumes the first CLI session, confirmed external-hook CLI cron runs preserve non-owner sender scope for normal and repair calls, and confirmed the local changed-file check stack passed.
- Edge cases checked: empty payloads without descendants, repair success, repair failure, CLI repair-mode bundled MCP disablement, and first CLI session reuse during repair.
- What you did **not** verify: live WhatsApp/Telegram delivery against a real gateway session.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Cron-owned CLI agent runs that produce no deliverable final text now perform one repair pass and record the repaired final text instead of silently ending without a user-visible result. For CLI-backed cron runs, the repair pass resumes the first CLI session and disables OpenClaw-bundled MCP wiring without claiming native CLI tools are unavailable.

Real environment tested: Windows/PowerShell checkout. The live repair proof below was run at repair head `0f1b25fbfc`; current pushed head `d6ed463c96` keeps that repair path, additionally restores external-hook CLI non-owner sender scope, includes the upstream lint repair needed for the final CI head, and addresses the Greptile duplicate-descendant-predicate review note; verified with targeted red/green tests plus `pnpm lint --threads=8`. Local OpenClaw Gateway was at `ws://127.0.0.1:19162` with isolated `OPENCLAW_HOME`; registered `codex-cli` runtime used installed OpenAI Codex CLI `codex-cli 0.130.0-alpha.5` through a thin proof wrapper around `codex exec --output-last-message`; cron delivery was disabled intentionally with `--no-deliver`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```text
corepack pnpm openclaw gateway health --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --json

corepack pnpm openclaw cron add --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --name "PR60212 fixed repair marker proof" --at "2030-01-08T00:00:00Z" --keep-after-run --session isolated --message "Real behavior proof for PR 60212. If the prompt you are reading contains the phrase Repair it now, reply exactly OPENCLAW_CRON_REPAIR_OK. Otherwise return an empty final answer and do not output any visible text." --no-deliver --timeout-seconds 300 --model openai/gpt-5.5 --json

corepack pnpm openclaw cron run 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --expect-final --timeout 360000

corepack pnpm openclaw cron show 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --json

corepack pnpm openclaw cron runs --id 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --limit 5 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted>
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
$ corepack pnpm openclaw gateway health --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --json
{
  "ok": true,
  "eventLoop": { "degraded": false, "reasons": [] },
  "plugins": { "loaded": ["acpx", "browser", "canvas", "device-pair", "file-transfer", "memory-core", "openai", "phone-control", "talk-voice"], "errors": [] }
}

$ corepack pnpm openclaw cron run 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --expect-final --timeout 360000
{
  "ok": true,
  "enqueued": true,
  "runId": "manual:3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091:1778695539774:2"
}
```

commands.author: Takhoffman

target.title: fix: UI glitch: config is not visible
target.body:
## Summary

- Raw config JSON textarea was invisible when switching from Form to Raw mode in Settings.
- Root cause: `.config-content` container retained scroll position from Form mode, so the textarea rendered above the scrolled viewport.
- Fix: detect form-mode transitions in `renderConfig()` and reset `.config-content` scroll to top.

Fixes #94202

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

**Behavior addressed**: Raw config textarea is visible immediately after switching from Form to Raw mode.

**Real setup tested**:
- **Runtime**: Node.js v24.13.1, OpenClaw gateway dev (port 18789, token auth)
- **Browser**: Playwright Chromium (headless, 1280x720)
- **Language**: English

**Exact steps or command run after fix**:

```bash
# Start gateway
cd openclaw && pnpm gateway:watch &
sleep 20
curl -s --noproxy '*' http://127.0.0.1:18789/health

# Playwright verification
npx playwright-cli open "http://127.0.0.1:18789/chat?token=<token>&lang=en"
# Settings -> Config -> Advanced -> Raw -> Reveal -> screenshot
```

**After-fix evidence**:

![1-form-mode](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/sunlit-deng/openclaw/88ed722939/.github/pr-assets/1-form-mode.png)

![2-raw-mode](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/sunlit-deng/openclaw/88ed722939/.github/pr-assets/2-raw-mode.png)

```
textareaVisible: true
boundingBox:  {x:301, y:273.8, width:936, height:500}
viewport:      1280x720
```

```json
{"ok":true,"status":"live"}
```

**Observed result after the fix**: Raw config textarea renders in the visible viewport (y=273.8 in 720px-high window). No manual scroll needed after switching from Form to Raw mode.

**What was not tested**: Other settings sections — same `.config-content` container, scroll reset applies uniformly. Non-Chromium browsers not explicitly tested but the scroll API is standard.

## Tests and validation

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run ui/src/ui/views/config.browser.test.ts` — 26/26 pass
- "switches mode via the sidebar toggle" — Raw button click exercised
- "keeps sensitive raw config hidden until reveal" — textarea rendering validated

## Change details

`ui/src/ui/views/config.ts` (+21/-32 lines):

```typescript
// Scroll helper: target-based (nav clicks) with global fallback (form/raw toggle)
const resetContentScroll = (target: EventTarget | null) => {
  queueMicrotask(() => {
    const origin = target instanceof Element ? target : null;
    const content = origin
      ?.closest(".config-main")
      ?.querySelector<HTMLElement>(".config-content")
      ?? globalThis.document?.querySelector<HTMLElement>(".config-content");
    if (!content) return;
    if (typeof content.scrollTo === "function") {
      content.scrollTo({ top: 0, left: 0, behavior: "auto" });
      return;
    }
    content.scrollTop = 0;
    content.scrollLeft = 0;
  });
};

// In renderConfig():
if (lastFormModeForScroll !== null && lastFormModeForScroll !== formMode) {
  resetContentScroll(null);
}
lastFormModeForScroll = formMode;
```

**Before/after data flow:**

```
Before: User clicks "Raw" -> .config-content.scrollTop = Form-mode position
        -> textarea above viewport -> appears empty

After:  User clicks "Raw" -> mode change detected
        -> queueMicrotask(resetContentScroll)
        -> .config-content.scrollTo({top:0,left:0}) -> textarea visible
```

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`No`) — Scroll-position reset only.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`No`)

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`No`)

What is the highest-risk area?
- `resetContentScroll` uses `globalThis.document?.querySelector`. In SSR contexts without `document`, the `??` fallback returns early (safe no-op).

How is that risk mitigated?
- Optional chaining returns `undefined` when `document` absent. `lastFormModeForScroll !== null` guard prevents spurious scroll on initial render.

## Current review state

What is the next action?
- Maintainer review

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?
- CI checks

target.title: fix/agents-primary-model-selection-and-catalog-loading-41344
target.body: ## Summary - Fixes incorrect Primary Model selection when switching between agents in Agents > Overview. - Fixes partial / blank Primary Model dropdowns after refresh or first load by ensuring the model catalog is available on Agents Overview paths. - Adds targeted tests for both selection stability and catalog hydration behavior. - Keeps the fix scoped to the Agents Overview model-selection path. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [x] Refactor required for the fix - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Related #41344 - Related #46275 - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable) For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`. - Root cause: 1. The agent details subtree could reuse stale DOM/select state when switching agents, which caused the Primary Model select to drift toward the previously viewed agent's state. 2. Agents Overview depended on a shared model catalog that was not guaranteed to be loaded on refresh / first-load / overview-only paths, so the select could render before the catalog was ready and degrade into a partial or blank-looking list. - Missing detection / guardrail: - Existing coverage covered some selected-value behavior, but it did not cover model-catalog hydration timing or the refresh/overview path where the catalog starts empty and arrives later. - Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): - Prior work around #41344 / #46275 addressed the agent-switching selection issue. Revalidating that fix on latest `upstream/main` exposed a second, related lifecycle issue in Agents Overview model-catalog loading. - Why this regressed now: - The Overview UI depends on model-catalog data, but not all entry paths guaranteed that the catalog was loaded before rendering. - If unknown, what was ruled out: - Ruled out a pure selected-state-only explanation. The partial/blank dropdown after refresh was caused by catalog availability/lifecycle, not just DOM selection drift. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should have caught this. Otherwise write `N/A`. - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [x] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: - `ui/src/ui/views/agents.browser.test.ts` - `ui/src/ui/views/agents.test.ts` - `ui/src/ui/controllers/models.test.ts` - Scenario the test should lock in: - Switching between agents keeps the correct explicit Primary Model selected. - The inherit-default option text does not go blank. - When the model catalog is initially empty, Agents Overview shows a loading state instead of a misleading partial list. - When the catalog hydrates later, the full list appears without losing the selected value. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: - The bug spans both UI render behavior and shared model-catalog lifecycle, so a combination of focused controller/unit tests plus browser-level select behavior tests is the smallest reliable coverage. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): - Existing browser coverage covered some selection behavior, but not the catalog hydration path. - If no new test is added, why not: - N/A ## User-visible / Behavior Changes List user-visible changes (including defaults/config). If none, write `None`. - Switching between agents no longer causes the Primary Model select to show the wrong agent's value. - On refresh / first entry to Agents Overview, the Primary Model select no longer degrades into a misleading partial list. - While the model catalog is still loading, the UI now shows a loading state instead of pretending only a small subset of options exists. - Once the catalog loads, the full option list appears without losing the current selection. ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No - New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No - Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: - N/A ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: Windows 10 - Runtime/container: local dev environment - Model/provider: local UI validation only - Integration/channel (if any): N/A - Relevant config (redacted): standard Agents UI configuration with default and non-default agents ### Steps 1. Open Agents > Overview > Model Selection > Primary Model. 2. Switch between a default agent and a non-default agent. 3. Refresh the page and reopen the Primary Model select immediately. 4. Optionally wait for model-catalog hydration or switch away/back to Overview. ### Expected - The select reflects the correct explicit value for the current agent. - The inherit-default option text is stable. - Refresh / first-load does not show a misleading partial catalog. - Once the catalog is available, the full model list appears without losing the selected value. ### Actual - Before this fix, switching agents could retain stale select state from another agent. - Before this fix, refresh / first-load could show only fallback options (for example inherit/current) or appear blank until the catalog became available later. ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ### UI Evidence #### Before After refresh / first load, the Primary Model dropdown could show only fallback entries or appear blank. #### After After the fix, the Primary Model dropdown restores the full option list correctly after refresh / first load, without losing the selected value. ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: - Switched repeatedly between default and non-default agents and confirmed Primary Model stays tied to the current agent. - Refreshed the page and reopened Agents Overview to verify the select no longer falls into a misleading partial/blank state. - Confirmed catalog hydration restores the full list without losing the selected value. - Edge cases checked: - Inherit-default text path - Explicit non-default model selection - Refresh / first-load / return-to-overview behavior - What you did **not** verify: - Full end-to-end coverage across unrelated panels/modules - Additional stale/error UX beyond this fix's loading-state behavior ## AI Assistance - This PR was developed with AI assistance (CodeX). - I reviewed the resulting changes, reproduced the bug locally, and verified the final behavior manually. - Testing level: fully tested locally with targeted automated tests and manual UI verification. ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes - Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No - Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No - If yes, exact upgrade steps: - N/A ## Failure Recovery (if this breaks) - How to disable/revert this change quickly: - Revert this PR's two commits. - Files/config to restore: - `ui/src/ui/views/agents.ts` - `ui/src/ui/views/agents-panels-overview.ts` - `ui/src/ui/controllers/models.ts` - `ui/src/ui/app-render.ts` - `ui/src/ui/app-settings.ts` - `ui/src/ui/app-chat.ts` - Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: - Primary Model shows another agent's value after switching agents - Refresh / first-load shows only partial model options or a blank-looking select - Catalog hydration changes the list but drops the selected value ## Risks and Mitigations List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`. - Risk: - Multiple UI entry points now ensure model-catalog availability, which could increase repeated refresh attempts if not properly deduped. - Mitigation: - The loading/ensure logic is centralized in the shared model controller and covered by targeted tests. - Risk: - Keyed subtree remounting can discard unsaved local UI state when switching agents. - Mitigation: - That tradeoff is intentional here because correctness of agent-specific model selection is more important than preserving stale subtree state across agent switches.

target.title: feat(slack): add ignoreOtherMentions channel config
target.body:
## Summary

- Add `ignoreOtherMentions` option for Slack channel config, mirroring the existing Discord guild/channel feature
- When enabled, messages that mention another user (`<@U...>`) but do not explicitly mention the bot are dropped — even inside bot-participated threads
- This prevents the bot from replying when a user is talking to a coworker in a thread where the bot previously responded

## Slack vs Discord difference

Discord's `ignoreOtherMentions` checks `!wasMentioned && !implicitMention`. The Slack version intentionally omits the `!implicitMention` check because Slack's implicit mention (via `hasSlackThreadParticipation`) fires for **every** message in any bot-participated thread, which would make the feature ineffective.

## Config example

```json5
{
  channels: {
    slack: {
      channels: {
        "*": { ignoreOtherMentions: true },
      },
    },
  },
}
```

## Changed files

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/config/types.slack.ts` | Add `ignoreOtherMentions` to `SlackChannelConfig` |
| `src/config/zod-schema.providers-core.ts` | Add zod validation for the new field |
| `extensions/slack/src/monitor/channel-config.ts` | Wire through type + resolver |
| `extensions/slack/src/monitor/message-handler/prepare.ts` | Drop logic before mention gating |

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm build` passes
- [x] `pnpm test -- extensions/slack/src/monitor` — 236 tests pass
- [x] Manual test on live Slack workspace: bot no longer replies when tagging a coworker in a bot thread
- [x] Bot still replies when explicitly @mentioned in the same thread

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: cron: reject webhook URLs with embedded credentials
target.body: ## Summary Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets: - Problem: Cron webhook URLs could include embedded credentials (e.g. https://user:pass@example.com/hook), which can leak secrets in logs, config, or URLs. - Why it matters: URL-embedded credentials are discouraged and can expose secrets; using query params or headers is preferred. - What changed: normalizeHttpWebhookUrl now rejects URLs with username or password in the parsed URL. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): No changes to auth headers, query params, or other credential handling; only http(s)://user:pass@host URLs are rejected. ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [ ] Docs - [x] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes # - Related # ## User-visible / Behavior Changes Cron webhook URLs with embedded credentials (e.g. https://user:secret@host/path) are now rejected as invalid. URLs without credentials continue to work. ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? (`No`) - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`) - New/changed network calls? (`No`) - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`) - Data access scope changed? (`No`) - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS (darwin) - Runtime/container: Node 22+ - Model/provider: N/A - Integration/channel (if any): CRON webhook delivery - Relevant config (redacted): N/A ### Steps 1. Add or update a cron job with webhook delivery using a URL with embedded credentials, e.g. https://user:pass@example.invalid/webhook. 2. Observe that the webhook URL is rejected (job will not use it). 3. Use the same job with https://example.invalid/webhook (no credentials) and confirm it is accepted. ### Expected - URLs with user:pass@ in the authority are rejected. - URLs without embedded credentials continue to work. ### Actual - Behavior matches above ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after - [x] Trace/log snippets - [ ] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: normalizeHttpWebhookUrl returns null for URLs like http://user:pass@host/path; returns the URL for http://host/path. - Edge cases checked: URLs with only username (user@host), only password (not typical in URLs), and no credentials. - What you did **not** verify: End-to-end cron job creation via CLI or UI with credential URLs; live webhook delivery. ## Review Conversations - [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? (`No`) - Config/env changes? (`No`) - Migration needed? (`Yes`) - If yes, exact upgrade steps: Update any cron job webhook URLs that include user:pass@ in the URL. Remove embedded credentials and use headers or query params for auth instead. ## Failure Recovery (if this breaks) - How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert this commit. - Files/config to restore: src/cron/webhook-url.ts - Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: Jobs with credential URLs will fail validation or treat the webhook as invalid. ## Risks and Mitigations List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`. - Risk: Existing jobs using credential URLs will break. - Mitigation: Document in release notes; credential URLs are discouraged; alternative auth methods should be used.

target.title: fix(mattermost): keep status read-only for SecretRef tokens
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `openclaw status` text mode crashed when Mattermost `botToken` used an unresolved `exec:keychain` SecretRef.
- Why it matters: Mattermost could be healthy at runtime, but read-only diagnostics failed before printing the channel table.
- What changed: Added a Mattermost read-only account inspector that reports configured-but-unavailable credential metadata without dereferencing the secret, and taught status snapshots to preserve that state.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): Runtime Mattermost token resolution remains strict; this only changes read-only inspection/status behavior.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #70942
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Mattermost had strict runtime account resolution but no read-only `inspectAccount` path. Status rendering fell back to `resolveAccount`, which throws on unresolved SecretRefs.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No Mattermost regression test covered read-only status inspection with an unresolved SecretRef credential.
- Contributing context (if known): Telegram, Discord, and Slack already had read-only inspectors; Mattermost was missing the equivalent path.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test

target.title: fix(cron): drop unenforceable default toolsAllow cap on CLI-backed runs
target.body:
## Problem

#91499 auto-stamps the creator's tool surface as a default `toolsAllow` cap on `agentTurn` cron payloads whenever the creating session is tool-restricted (a narrowing allow-policy or an explicit deny). CLI backends cannot enforce a runtime `toolsAllow` — `cli-runner/prepare.ts` rejects any defined allow-list — so every scheduled `agentTurn` that resolves to a CLI backend (e.g. `claude-cli`) fails to start. This silently broke per-thread scheduled continuations on CLI backends.

## Fix

A CLI backend is not a runtime tool-policy boundary: it runs with its own configured tool set, as the operator, on the local machine, and refuses a runtime allow-list outright. An inherited default cap is therefore unenforceable on a CLI backend. Decide at run time, where the backend is known:

- **Flag the default.** `capCronAgentTurnToolsAllow` stamps `toolsAllowIsDefault` when it fills in the creator surface because the cron requested nothing (or a bare `*`). An explicit narrowing or empty allow-list is a real per-cron restriction and carries no flag.
- **Drop only the default, only on CLI.** The run-executor drops a flagged default in the CLI branch and lets the run proceed. An explicit per-cron restriction (no flag) is deliberately passed through, so `prepare.ts` still fails it closed and surfaces that the requested policy needs an embedded runtime. Embedded runs are untouched and keep the full cap enforced.
- **Persist the flag.** New nullable `cron_jobs.payload_tools_allow_is_default` column (additive `ensureColumn` migration + codec read/write), plus `toolsAllowIsDefault` on the gateway-protocol `agentTurn` payload schema — the stamped payload is otherwise rejected by the contract's `additionalProperties: false`.
- **Preserve the flag across updates.** A no-`toolsAllow` update (reschedule, prompt edit) re-derives the default (flag intact) instead of carrying the stored value forward as a literal — which previously routed it through the explicit-narrowing branch, stripped the flag, and re-broke the job on CLI after the next restart.

Net policy: on CLI only the unenforceable inherited default is relaxed; explicit per-cron restrictions still fail closed; embedded backends are unchanged.

## Verification

- `tsgo:core` clean; `oxlint` clean on changed files.
- 683 passing across the cron + store + isolated-agent suites, including new coverage: run-executor drops the flagged default but propagates an explicit restriction on CLI; cron-tool stamps/clears the flag across create and update and preserves it across a no-`toolsAllow` update; store round-trips the flag (and its absence) through SQLite.

## Real-behaviour proof — BEFORE (the regression, live)

Captured on a production `claude-cli` gateway running the pre-fix build, by force-running an `agentTurn` cron that carries a `toolsAllow` cap:

- **Scenario:** `agentTurn` cron, `toolsAllow` cap stamped by capCron (sent `["exec"]`, capped to `[]`), forced run, resolves to the `claude-cli` backend.
- **Result:** run errors in **205 ms, before any agent work**:
  `Error: CLI backend claude-cli cannot enforce runtime toolsAllow; use an embedded runtime for restricted tool policy`
- **Mechanism:** the cap reaches `cli-runner/prepare.ts`, which rejects any defined runtime `toolsAllow` → the scheduled continuation never starts. This is the exact failure the fix removes for the *default* (unenforceable) cap.

AFTER behaviour (run-executor drops the flagged default on CLI and the run proceeds) is covered by `run.message-tool-policy.test.ts`; a filmed before/after cron-wake capture (reply landing back in the Telegram topic) is being assembled as a follow-up artifact.

## Risk / mitigation

- **Risk:** a reloaded job loses the in-memory flag and re-hits the rejection. **Mitigation:** the flag is persisted (SQLite column + wire-schema field) and round-tripped by the codec; preserved across no-`toolsAllow` updates.
- **Not covered:** `agentTurn` crons created during the regression window carry a flagless `toolsAllow` and remain fail-closed on CLI until recreated or updated with an explicit `toolsAllow`.

target.title: fix(discord): use configured statusReactions.timing instead of DEFAULT_TIMING
target.body:
## Summary

Makes the Discord message finalizer use user-configured `statusReactions.timing.doneHoldMs` / `errorHoldMs` instead of hardcoded `DEFAULT_TIMING` values, matching Telegram and Slack parity.

## Linked context

Closes #78431

Related: #81623 (previous attempt closed as stale — this PR preserves `finalDeliveryFailed` handling and adds focused tests)

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** The Discord message finalizer uses the configured `statusReactions.timing.doneHoldMs` / `errorHoldMs` instead of `DEFAULT_TIMING`. Terminal cleanup now waits for the configured hold, not the hardcoded 1500ms / 2500ms defaults.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout, Windows 11, Node 24.16.0, pnpm 11.7.0, Discord plugin isolated vitest run with fake timers.

**Exact steps run after this patch:**

1. **RED — prove the strengthened regression catches the old default behavior.** Reverted the runtime fix in `message-handler.process.ts` (back to `DEFAULT_TIMING.doneHoldMs` / `errorHoldMs`), then ran the two configured-timing tests:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.process.test.ts -t "uses configured statusReactions.timing"
```

```
 ❯ extension-discord  ../../extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.process.test.ts (109 tests | 2 failed | 107 skipped) 3147ms
 ⎯⎯⎯ Failed Tests 2 ⎯⎯⎯
 Test Files  1 failed (1)
      Tests  2 failed | 107 skipped (109)
```

Both strengthened tests fail at the new `not.toHaveBeenCalledWith(..., DEFAULT_EMOJIS.done/error, ...)` assertion at the old-default window — because with the old `DEFAULT_TIMING` the cleanup already fired by 1500ms (done) / 2500ms (error). This confirms the test now proves the runtime fix, not just the eventual cleanup.

2. **GREEN — re-applied the fix and ran the full vitest file (no `-t` filter):**

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.process.test.ts
```

```
 ✓ extension-discord  extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.process.test.ts (109 tests) 15171ms
     ✓ carries preflight audio transcript into dispatch context and marks media transcribed 1918ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  109 passed (109)
   Start at  00:45:32
   Duration  235.85s (transform 164.67s, setup 4.55s, import 214.48s, tests 15.17s, environment 0ms)

[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 261.50s
```

**Evidence after fix:** 109/109 tests pass, including the two strengthened regression tests that assert the done/error reaction is **not** removed at the old `DEFAULT_TIMING` window (1500ms / 2500ms) and **is** removed only after the configured hold (2000ms / 4000ms) elapses.

**Observed result after fix:** Configured `doneHoldMs: 2000` is honored — the done emoji is held past the old 1500ms default and cleaned up only at 2000ms. Configured `errorHoldMs: 4000` is honored — the error emoji is held past the old 2500ms default and cleaned up only at 4000ms.

**What was not tested:** Live Discord guild. Not needed for this fix: the bug is purely about which `setTimeout` value the finalizer selects, and the fake-timer regression now proves the selected value is the configured one (not the default) by asserting no cleanup at the old default window. Live Discord would only re-confirm the same `setTimeout` selection the unit test already pins.

## Tests and validation

**Strengthened regression tests (addresses ClawSweeper [P2] review finding):**

The previous test advanced straight to `2_000` ms and then checked that cleanup happened. Current main already removes the done reaction at `1_500` ms, so that eventual assertion would pass **without** the runtime fix. The strengthened tests now:

1. Advance to `DEFAULT_TIMING.doneHoldMs` (1500ms) and assert `removeReactionDiscord` was **not** called with `DEFAULT_EMOJIS.done` — fails on old default, passes with fix.
2. Advance the remaining window to the configured `2_000` ms and assert cleanup **is** called.
3. A matching `errorHoldMs` test drives the error terminal path via `failedCounts: { final: 1 }`, asserts no removal at `DEFAULT_TIMING.errorHoldMs` (2500ms), then removal at the configured `4_000` ms.

**Gate commands run locally:**

```bash
# Formatting (the only file changed is the test file)
node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 \
  extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.process.test.ts \
  extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.process.ts
# → All matched files use the correct format.

# Test type check (G17: *.test.ts changed)
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json \
  --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions-test.tsbuildinfo
# → exit 0, no type errors

# Full vitest file (no -t filter)
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.process.test.ts
# → 109 passed
```

## Risk checklist

| Question | Yes/No |
| -------- | ------ |
| Is this a breaking change? | No |
| Does this add a new dependency? | No |
| Does this change user-facing config or API surface? | No |
| Does this affect other channels (Telegram, Slack)? | No |

Low risk. The runtime change is isolated to Discord's terminal sleep value selection. The compatibility note (existing non-default Discord `statusReactions.timing` configs now affect visible reaction lifetime) matches the documented contract and Telegram/Slack behavior.

## Current review state

Addressed ClawSweeper [P2] review finding (`extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.process.test.ts:1306-1307`): the regression test now asserts no cleanup at the old `DEFAULT_TIMING` window before advancing to the configured hold, so CI proves the runtime fix rather than passing against the old default. Added a matching `errorHoldMs` regression test. Ready for `@clawsweeper re-review`.
